title: Westpac investor loans plunge 20pc on federal budget tax shock
full_text:
The government's proposed reforms to negative gearing and capital gains tax have triggered a 20 per cent fall in Westpac's housing investor loan applications over the past three weeks, with the head of consumer banking Carolyn McCann warning there is widespread community concern about the changes.
The federal budget revealed a minimum 30 per cent tax on capital gains to be imposed from July 2027 and the abolition of negative gearing for existing properties. Both changes rattled investors and caused a slide in auction clearance rates and house prices in Melbourne and Sydney.
"I would call it a mood of concern rather than crisis," said McCann ahead of a strategy briefing on Thursday.
The housing market was already subdued after the Reserve Bank of Australia raised official interest rates three times this year and the Middle East conflict in February sent fuel prices soaring and hurt household budgets.
"When you put the three together, it's quite a lot of change," McCann said of the budget tax changes, Middle East conflict and interest rate rises. "Customers and the community could be a bit concerned about the changing signals."
House prices have already fallen in Sydney and Melbourne, while Westpac predicts they will decline overall by 3 per cent in Sydney and 4 per cent in Melbourne this year.
However, other forecasters predict the pullback could be sharper. Morgan Stanley, for example, expects house values to fall by up to 10 per cent nationally after the budget brought the housing super cycle to a halt.
"High debt-to-income caps will prevent first home buyers from filling the investor gap, while some upgraders may take a wait-and-see approach given the potential for prices to fall 5 per cent to 10 per cent," said Morgan Stanley's banking analyst Richard Wiles this week.
McCann said many people were sitting on the sidelines to see what would happen, but supply-side constraints would eventually create a floor to any price declines.
"The dynamics of the market mean it won't be a dramatic decline," she said. "It will be a slowdown, and then pick up again. We are seeing owner-occupied holding up. The grandfathering of existing arrangements [with negative gearing] and exemption for new builds will soften the decline."
Still, investors in bank shares have been spooked, with hedge funds doubling their short positions in Australian banks over the past six months to a record level. Shares in Westpac are down just over 5 per cent in the past month.
The drop-off in loan applications from housing investors who will no longer be able to negatively gear future property purchases supports Westpac's forecast for the housing credit growth to fall from 6.5 per cent this financial year to 4.7 per cent in the 2027 financial year. It expects credit growth to then bounce back to 5.2 per cent in the 2028 financial year.
McCann, who has been in her role for almost a year, will brief analysts on Thursday morning on her retail banking strategy alongside Luis Uguina, who was recently recruited from Macquarie. Uguina helped build Macquarie's retail banking technology systems and Westpac hired him to lead data and AI in its consumer bank. Westpac wants to improve its use of data and its banking app by creating more personalised customer experiences to tackle competitors Macquarie and Commonwealth Bank.
Westpac will also target the acquisition of more customers who are skilled migrants and younger people saving for their first home, cohorts that CBA has historically dominated.
However, only one-third of Westpac's retail customers call it their "main financial institution". This reflects a relatively large share of home loans being sourced by mortgage brokers - about 60 per cent - which the bank wants to reduce to half. As well, 40 per cent of Westpac customers only have one product with the bank and McCann hopes to improve this figure through more cross-selling.
McCann also said Westpac was increasing its competitive position in deposits, amid increased competition from rivals such as Macquarie. She said the proportion of Westpac customers who received bonus interest rates on their accounts had risen from 75 per cent five years ago to 86 per cent today.
"It's been competitive for quite some time and Macquarie has brought in a different dynamic."
Last week, Westpac lifted the age limit on its "Spend&Save" account from 34 to 40. The maximum balance eligible for the bonus rate was also increased from $30,000 to $150,000, to align with the average age of a first home buyer and the size of a deposit. The moves are a bid to win market share from CBA and Macquarie among younger savers, who could then become mortgage customers.
"The thinking there is we're underrepresented in that part of the market: that age bracket is only about 13 or 14 per cent of our portfolio," McCann said. "We believe that's a core part of the book we could be doing better in. So, we'll be quite deliberate in how and where we compete."
In terms of the bank's growth ambition, McCann said Westpac wants to grow mortgages at the average rate of the system each month, and was targeting 1.1 times system growth for deposits.
She acknowledged Westpac was slower than rivals to boost investment in "first party", or proprietary, channels after following CBA in boosting pay to retain mortgage bankers who had been leaving. The number of loans written directly by the main Westpac brand is now 38 per cent and the goal is to lift this by 2 per cent each half until it hits 50 per cent.
With Westpac making 1500 staff redundant to help reduce a bloated cost base, McCann said jobs in her retail division had been culled by around 400 over the past year and declined to specify whether additional cuts would follow. "We've done a lot of heavy lifting already," she said, pointing to another 50 mortgage lenders to be hired in the next few months.
Fairfax Media Management Pty Limited
author: James Eyers
heading: Banking and Economy
